sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Q fever is a zoonotic infection caused by *Coxiella burnetii*, an obligate intracellular bacterium. It is endemic in pastoral and farming regions of Australia and is particularly relevant for mine sites adjacent to sheep, cattle, kangaroo, or feral pig populations.

**MANDATORY NOTIFIABLE DISEASE in Western Australia**

**Occupational notifiable disease** - workplace notification required if acquired at work.

**Transmission**
- **Inhalation** of contaminated aerosols (dried birth fluids, placenta, manure, soil, wool) - primary route
- Extremely low infectious dose - **one organism may cause infection**
- Contact with infected animals or carcasses
- Unpasteurised milk (rare)
- **NOT transmitted person to person**

**Mine Site Context**
- Mine sites adjacent to pastoral land, abattoirs, or with feral animal activity
- Earthworks, road construction, or dust generation in contaminated areas
- Workers who hunt or camp in pastoral areas during leave
- Dust storms from farming areas can carry organism long distances

**Risk Factors for Severe Disease**
- Pre-existing cardiac valvular disease, vascular aneurysm, or prosthetic valves - risk of endocarditis
- Pregnancy - high risk of adverse outcomes
- Immunosuppression

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Acute Q Fever (Most Common)**
- Abrupt onset after 2–3 weeks incubation
- **High fever** (38–40°C), often with rigors
- **Severe headache** (often frontal - characteristic)
- Profuse sweating
- Myalgia, arthralgia, fatigue
- Dry cough, mild chest discomfort

**Presentations within acute Q fever:**
| Syndrome | Features |
|---|---|
| Flu-like (most common) | Self-limiting 2–3 weeks, fever, headache, myalgia |
| Pneumonia | Dry cough, dyspnoea, bilateral infiltrates |
| Hepatitis | Right upper quadrant pain, elevated LFTs, jaundice |
| Combined | Any combination of above |

**Chronic Q Fever (Rare but Serious)**
- **Endocarditis** - presents months to years after acute infection; fever, heart murmur, embolic events
- **Vascular infection** - aortic aneurysm infection
- Fatigue syndrome
- High mortality if not treated

**Q Fever Fatigue Syndrome**
- Prolonged fatigue, musculoskeletal pain, neuropsychological symptoms after acute Q fever
- Can significantly impair work capacity for months

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
**Treatment - Doxycycline Required**
- Standard antibiotics (penicillin, most cephalosporins, aminoglycosides) are **ineffective**
- First-line: **doxycycline 100 mg BD × 14 days** for acute Q fever
- Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin) are an alternative
- Chronic Q fever (endocarditis): prolonged combination therapy (months to years) - specialist management

**Pregnancy - Special Caution**
- Doxycycline is contraindicated in pregnancy
- Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole used instead
- Q fever in pregnancy associated with spontaneous abortion, intrauterine growth restriction, premature labour
- Refer urgently

**Vaccination - Q-VAX**
- An effective Q fever vaccine (Q-VAX) is available in Australia
- **Pre-vaccination screening is mandatory** - previous infection or vaccination causes severe local reactions if re-vaccinated
- Pre-vaccination protocol: skin test (intradermal) + serology before vaccination
- Recommended for all at-risk workers in pastoral/mining areas
- Arrange through occupational health

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
**Immediate Assessment**
- Vital signs: temperature, heart rate, blood pressure, SpO2, respiratory rate
- Assess for pneumonia (SpO2, respiratory rate, chest auscultation)
- History: recent exposure to livestock, farming areas, feral animals, dust storms

**Treatment**
**Doxycycline (first-line):**
- 100 mg PO BD × 14 days for acute uncomplicated Q fever
- Take with a full glass of water; remain upright for 30 minutes

**If pneumonia:**
- Oxygen: titrate to SpO2 >94%
- Doxycycline continues
- Evacuation if SpO2 <94% on room air, respiratory rate >25/min, or haemodynamically unstable

**Notification**
- Notify WA Health: (08) 9222 4177
- Complete occupational incident notification

**Symptomatic Management**
- Paracetamol or ibuprofen for fever and headache
- Adequate hydration
- Rest

**Evacuation Indications**
- Pneumonia with SpO2 <94%
- Jaundice or hepatitis with liver failure signs
- Suspected chronic Q fever (new cardiac murmur, embolic events)
- Pregnancy

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
**Pneumonia with Respiratory Failure**
- High-flow oxygen; NIPPV if cooperative and haemodynamically stable
- Doxycycline IV if unable to take oral (200 mg IV day 1, then 100 mg IV BD)
- Intubation if GCS declining or respiratory failure unresponsive to oxygen

**Endocarditis / Chronic Q Fever**
- Suspected if: known previous Q fever + new cardiac murmur, embolic signs, prolonged fever
- Requires urgent evacuation and specialist management
- Treatment: doxycycline + hydroxychloroquine for 18 months minimum (valvular disease)

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
**Post-Q Fever Fatigue Syndrome**
- Persistent fatigue after acute Q fever is common (up to 20%)
- Investigate and exclude other causes
- Graded activity program - see post-viral-fatigue-and-graded-recovery guideline

**Workplace Response**
- Identify source of exposure if possible
- Check vaccination status of all exposed workers
- Consider serology testing of other workers with similar exposure history

**Return to Work**
- Uncomplicated acute Q fever: can usually return to work after 2–3 weeks with completed antibiotic course and symptom resolution
- Document return to work clearly
